story:
The alarm blares at 6:00 a.m. I hit it and sit up, chest heavy, body sore. I pull off the comforter and cross the room to the dress that’s been hanging on my door for three weeks. I hold it and wonder why. Why this dress. Why this day.

I slide it on carefully, easing the seams over my hips, then my shoulders. I tug the corset ribbons until the fabric holds me firm and breathing feels tight. Why am I doing this? I tie the bows and move to my hair.

I curl each section slow and neat. The iron grazes my neck; I barely react. Soft waves brush my waist. I twist half up into a neat bun. I don’t want makeup—it will wash away later—but I dust on blush and a little highlighter anyway. Light. Easy to hide.

It’s 7:45. The ceremony is at 8:30. Who gets married this early? A horn honks outside. Tessa. I don’t need to look. I walk to her car and feel my heart grow heavier with each step. She tells me she knows how hard this is. She doesn’t. She can’t.

We reach the venue just before 8:20. Guests file in. When the music starts, I walk down the aisle on the arm of the best man. For a second, I pretend the aisle is mine. That I’m the bride. That I’m walking toward Luke.

We take our places. The bridal song swells. Angela appears, glowing. My twin. So beautiful. All eyes are on her. Mine are, too—heavy with envy. I’ve always envied her. She’s prettier, kinder, smarter. She shines. Why couldn’t I be loved like that? Even our parents favored her, praising every step while I got the lectures. Luke used to swear nothing would come between us. He said he’d never be with someone like her. He said that. What changed?

The priest speaks. I drift until the vows pull me back. Luke’s voice is steady: he’ll care for her in sickness and in health, she’s his one true love, the future mother of his children, the best thing that’s ever happened to him. No one else compares. He can’t wait to be hers forever. Each word lands like a sting.

Memories press in. Our childhood. Hours spent together. How he gave me a ring for my fourteenth birthday—a promise ring, he said, to promise we’d always be best friends. I hoped it meant more. I wished it meant more. But somewhere along the way, he chose Angela. He loves Angela. I wonder what life would be if it were me in her place—me in the white dress, me hearing those vows. I’ve wanted one thing for as long as I can remember: for Luke to choose me. To love me back.

“You may now kiss the bride,” the priest says, and the spell breaks. I want to object. I want to move. I can’t. I watch him kiss her. The pain is sharp and quiet. The rest of the day slips by in a blur I can’t hold onto.

The next morning, only one truth remains: I never told him how I felt. I sob into my pillow, and deep down I admit the thing I’ve always known.

It was never going to be me.
